Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD only contains variable assignments and array definitions at the top level. No `pkgver()` function is defined, so no code executes during sourcing beyond simple string operations. The `source` arrays point to GitHub (raw.githubusercontent.com and github.com/releases) using HTTPS, which is the official upstream for this package. All source entries have pinned SHA-256 checksums, ensuring integrity. There are no dangerous commands (curl|bash, eval, base64, etc.) in the top-level scope. The `package()` function is not executed by the requested commands (`--nobuild --nodeps --noprepare`). Therefore, fetching sources and sourcing the PKGBUILD is safe.
</details>
<summary>No malicious code in top-level scope; safe to fetch sources.</summary>
</security_assessment>

It downloads the upstream binary from the official GitHub releases page with pinned checksums (SHA256) for each architecture and the documentation/license files. The `package()` function merely installs the binary into `/usr/bin/`, documentation into `/usr/share/doc/`, and the license into `/usr/share/licenses/`. There are no suspicious network calls, no obfuscated code, no use of `eval`, `curl|bash`, or any unexpected file operations. The build does not execute any custom scripts or modify system configuration. The file is consistent with legitimate packaging and shows no signs of a supply-chain attack.
</details>
<summary>Standard binary PKGBUILD with pinned checksums, no malicious behavior.</summary>

Cloning https://aur.archlinux.org/aurelia-bin.git to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-ntaixxpc...

Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines variables and arrays for use in package construction but does not execute any harmful code during the sourcing phase. Running `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` will source the PKGBUILD and evaluate the `pkgver()` function (if present), but in this case, `pkgver` is hardcoded and no `pkgver()` function is defined, so no dynamic version check occurs.

The `source` array retrieves files from the expected GitHub repository of the project using predictable URLs based on the declared `_gitauthor`, `_gitname`, and `_gitversion`. The URLs are constructed using static variables and point to official release binaries and documentation files (README, USAGE, LICENSE), which is a standard and safe practice. There are no command substitutions, `eval` calls, or remote code executions at the top level or within `pkgver`.

Additionally, the `package()` function is not executed under `--noprepare --nobuild`, so its contents are out of scope. The download of pre-built binaries from GitHub releases is normal for `-bin` packages, and checksums are provided for all sources. While downloading pre-compiled binaries carries inherent trust in the maintainer/upstream, this is not a concern for the narrow scope of source retrieval and sourcing the PKGBUILD.

Thus, simply sourcing this PKGBUILD and printing srcinfo does not trigger any malicious behavior.
</details>
<summary>
No dangerous code in sourcing or source retrieval.
</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- No dangerous code in sourcing or source retrieval.[/]

LLM auditresponse for LICENSE-0.1.11:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file provided is a license document (LICENSE-0.1.11) for the `aurelia-bin` package. It contains a mix of copyright statements and the full text of the GNU General Public License v3 (GPLv3). The content outlines licensing transitions in the project's history, acknowledging prior work from SteamFlow and specifying that commits after a certain point are licensed under GPLv3.

There is no executable code, network activity, file manipulation, or system interaction of any kind in this file. It serves purely as a legal and informational document. The presence of the full GPLv3 license text is standard practice and poses no security risk. The copyright notices and references to external projects (like SteamFlow) are normal in open-source projects and do not constitute malicious behavior.

The file contains only static text and is consistent with standard software licensing practices. It does not execute commands, download payloads, or access system resources. Therefore, it presents no supply-chain threat.
</details>
<summary>
License file with no executable or malicious content</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/2] Reviewing PKGBUILD...
+ Reviewed LICENSE-0.1.11. Status: SAFE -- License file with no executable or malicious content
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines a package for a precompiled Rust application, sourcing binaries directly from GitHub releases of the upstream project's repository. The source URLs point to tagged releases under the project's official GitHub account, and the corresponding binaries are checksummed using known-good sha256 values listed in the file. This ensures integrity and guards against tampering.

The build process is straightforward and appropriate for a binary package: it places the prebuilt binary into the correct location under /usr/bin/, along with documentation and license files. No compilation or dynamic code generation occurs. The package follows standard AUR conventions, including the use of !strip (common for prebuilt binaries) and appropriate file installations.

There are no signs of malicious activity: no obfuscated code, no unauthorized network requests, and no use of dangerous commands like eval, curl|bash, or unexpected file operations. The application’s purpose — a Steam launcher — may involve network interactions by design, but the packaging itself does not introduce risks. The use of SKIP is not present; all sources are properly checksummed.
</details>
<summary>
Standard prebuilt binary package with proper checksums and safe installation</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for README-0.1.11.md:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided file is a README markdown document for the Aurelia project, which describes a command-line Steam launcher written in Rust. It contains no executable code, scripts, or package build instructions. The content is purely informational, outlining project features, usage instructions, prerequisites, and licensing.

Security analysis reveals no malicious elements: there are no network requests, file operations, or system commands embedded within the markdown. The document mentions that the application modifies Steam's files directly, which is a functional disclaimer rather than a security threat in the context of the AUR package itself. This behavior is expected for a Steam library manager and is properly disclosed in the disclaimer.

The README references legitimate dependencies and protocols (like `steam-vent`), and while it describes downloading components like Luxtorpeda on demand, this behavior is opt-in, transparently documented, and occurs at runtime under user control—not during package installation. None of these aspects constitute supply-chain attacks or malicious behavior in the packaging context.
</details>
<summary>
Documentation-only file, no executable code present.
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/3] Reviewing .nvchecker.toml, USAGE-0.1.11.md...
+ Reviewed README-0.1.11.md. Status: SAFE -- Documentation-only file, no executable code present.
LLM auditresponse for USAGE-0.1.11.md:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided file is a documentation markdown file (USAGE-0.1.11.md) for the Aurelia CLI tool, which is a legitimate Steam launcher for Linux/Unix systems. It details the usage of various commands for managing Steam games, authentication, library management, game installation, and more. 

The document contains no executable code, scripts, or network requests. It is purely descriptive, outlining how users can interact with the Aurelia binary through command-line arguments. There are no signs of obfuscation, malicious payloads, backdoors, or unauthorized data exfiltration.

All described functionality aligns with the legitimate purpose of a Steam library manager: authenticating with Steam, listing and installing games, managing Proton versions, launching titles, handling Steam Cloud sync, and interacting with Steam features like Workshop, friends, chat, inventory, and market data. The use of environment variables like `AURELIA_CONFIG_DIR` and `RUST_LOG` is standard for configuration and debugging in Rust-based command-line tools.

No external domains, suspicious URLs, or unauthorized network endpoints are referenced beyond expected Steam services (e.g., Steam Community, Steam CDN). The file poses no security risk as it is only documentation.
</details>
<summary>
Documentation only, no executable code or malicious content.
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[2/3] Reviewing .nvchecker.toml...
+ Reviewed USAGE-0.1.11.md. Status: SAFE -- Documentation only, no executable code or malicious content.
LLM auditresponse for .nvchecker.toml:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file `.nvchecker.toml` is a configuration file for nvchecker, a tool used to monitor upstream software versions. It specifies that the version for `aurelia-bin` should be tracked from the GitHub repository `Drackrath/Aurelia`, using the latest release and expecting version tags prefixed with "v". This is a common and reasonable use of nvchecker, particularly in AUR packages that track upstream releases.

There are no executable commands, network operations, or file manipulations performed directly by this file. The configuration only defines a source and method for version checking, which is a passive and read-only operation. The repository specified matches the expected upstream for the package name, and no obfuscated or suspicious data is present.

Such configuration files are routinely used by AUR maintainers to automate version bumps and are not inherently dangerous. The content aligns with standard and safe AUR packaging practices.
</details>
<summary>
Configuration file for version tracking; no malicious content
</summary>
</security_assessment>
